data.title: Apollo's APOS: Good Investment If Redeemed, Great If Not
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

With this short article, we would like to show our readers the latest fixed-income portfolio addition and share some reasons why potential investors can consider it worthy of their attention. The security in question is the Apollo Global Management, Inc.'s (NYSE:APO) Reset Rate Junior Subordinated Notes due 09/15/2053 (NYSE:APOS). The recent drop in treasury yields, accompanied by the widening of the credit spreads of corporate fixed-income securities, has opened some attractive investment possibilities. APOS is a high-quality product that offers a lucrative yield, and we will try to defend this thesis now.

APOS metrics

All the relevant information regarding the debt issue can be found in its prospectus. Here we will point out only the most important metrics for us that we will use in the analysis that follows.

APOS will pay a 7.625% annual interest in the form of $0.4766 quarterly distributions until its first possible redemption date on 12/15/2028. After that, the annual interest will be equal to the five-year US Treasury rate on the applicable reset calculation date plus a fixed spread of 3.226%, resetting every five years. The stated maturity date for the security is 9/15/2053. APOS is credit-rated BBB+ by S&P, A3 by Moody's, and BBB+ by Fitch.

The daily price chart of the issue looks like this:

Currently, APOS is trading at a $26.04 price with a Yield-To-Call metric of 6.54%. If not called, the bond will reset its interest on 12/15/2028 and every five years after that with the 3.226% spread over the five-year Treasuries at that time. There's no way for us to know what the forward yield curve in general and the five-year Treasuries, in particular, will look like for such a long period as 2053 presents, so we cannot give a fair estimation of the yield-to-maturity metric for APOS.

The forward yield curve depicted above gives us an expected value of 4.52% for the five-year Treasury yield at December 2028, when the first call date/interest adjustment for APOS will be. Based on this, if the security is not redeemed, its annual interest will be adjusted to 7.73%, or around 0.50% higher than the fixed rate of 7.25% at the moment. At the moment, the expectation for the five-year Treasury yield in 2033 is to be higher than at the reset date in 2028. The expected trend is clear, and should something completely unexpected happen to the yield curve, we anticipate APOS to trade to its call date as a low-duration debt issue.

The Company

Founded in 1990 and headquartered in New York, Apollo Global Management is a leading provider of global alternative asset management solutions and retirement services. The company's clients vary from individuals to institutions, and in its task to seek excess returns for them, Apollo invests in private and public markets across debt, equity, and real assets. The corporation's line of business is conducted primarily in the US through three major segments: Asset Management, Retirement Services, and Principal Investing.

The daily chart below shows how the market evaluates the common stock of Apollo Global Management — APO:

Dividends

APO has 13 consecutive years of uninterrupted dividend payments, as the last two of them were with consecutive dividend increases. This is what the dividend history of the company looks like:

The twelve-month trailing dividend distributed to Apollo Global Management's common shareholders amounts to $1.85, in quarter distributions of $0.4625. With the current price of the stock at $138.37, that corresponds to a dividend yield of 1.34%. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past ten years:

And the forward estimations:

Apollo Global Management has approximately 569 million common shares outstanding. With a $1.85 TTM dividend per share, the company has distributed $1.05 billion in common shares dividends over the past twelve months. According to the company's end-of-year report, it has $1.85 billion in interest expenses TTM. The common share dividends can cover the total interest payments for approximately half a year.

Capital Structure

Below is Apollo Global Management's capital structure as of the end-of-year filing for 2024. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is presented, too, for the sake of comparison in the short term.

As of Q4'24, Apollo Global Management had a total debt of $31.9 billion, senior to the preferred equity of $1.4 billion. At the lowest position of the capital structure is the common equity of $15.9 billion. This gives us a 1.84 debt-to-equity ratio. The charts show that Apollo's capital structure was quite stable over the past couple of quarters.

Credit Rating

Fitch Ratings - Chicago - 18 Oct 2024: Fitch Ratings has affirmed the Long-Term Issuer Default Ratings (IDRs) of Apollo Global Management, Inc. (AGM), Apollo Asset Management, Inc. (AAM) and its rated subsidiaries (collectively, Apollo) at 'A'. The Rating Outlook is Stable.

....

Global Investment Manager: The ratings affirmations reflect Apollo's strong competitive position as a global alternative IM, its experienced management team, solid investment track record, strong operating margins, relatively predictable fee earnings stream given peer-leading perpetual capital, modest leverage, solid liquidity and a below-average payout ratio.

This is the beginning of the rating action commentary with which Fitch affirms the 'A' long-term issuer default rating with a stable outlook of Apollo Global Management. The rating history of APO shows that, according to Fitch Ratings, there were no significant changes in the creditworthiness of the corporation in the last two years.

Apollo OTC bond issues

Above are the OTC traded bonds of Apollo Global Management with their respective yields and maturity dates. The senior unsecured debt issues of the company are rated 'A2' by Moody's or 'A' by S&P. The issue with maturity in '29 and last traded yield of 4.58% has the closest duration to APOS, so we'll use it as a comparison. All its relevant metrics are shown below:

The '29 OTC bond is two notches higher in credit rating than APOS, according to S&P. We expect APOS to trade with approximately 50 basis points higher YTC in order to be fairly priced to the senior debt issue, or with YTC of around 5.1-5.2%. That corresponds to a price above $27.20 for APOS.

Peer comparison

Apollo has no other debt issues besides APOS listed on the exchange, so we will only do the peer comparison.

For the peer comparison, we look at baby bonds, investment-grade rated by at least one of the big three credit rating agencies. The set is of debt issues, trading above par, before their respective call dates. The comparison is by the yield-to-call metric.

Below are the metrics of the securities. In this group, APOS has the best overall credit score.

The final comparison will be to the listed baby bonds in the "Investment Advice" SIC sector, shown below. This is a little tricky comparison, as the rest of the securities in this set are trading below par and are evaluated by their YTM. They have more upside potential and a much longer duration than APOS. If, however, APOS is not redeemed in 2028 and transitions to the reset rate, its yield will be superior to all the issues, while being of higher credit quality too.

The securities with their relevant metrics:

Conclusion

APOS is one of the latest additions to my portfolio. The issuing company is as stable as ever, and nothing at the moment indicates they will have any problem paying the interest on the debt issue. The security is undervalued compared to the OTC traded senior debt of Apollo. Whether evaluated as a short-term security that will be redeemed on its call date in 2028, or expected to transition to a reset rate, it is undervalued to its peers, most of which are of lower credit quality.

data.title: The Economic Pulse Of Solana: Price Bubble? Or Deep Value?
data.description:
The Economic Pulse of Solana: Price Bubble? Or Deep Value?

At the beginning of 2024, Solana USD (SOL-USD) was at a price of $101. Over the past year and part of this year, the asset has appreciated by an incredible 191% since that date, reaching a value of $295 on 20 January 2025. From that peak until today, the asset has depreciated by 55% in just 66 days. As can be seen in the graph below, the fall has been impressive. In this article, we will analyse whether both the appreciation and the fall were due to a purely speculative bubble or whether there was a chink of value in its network. We will dive into the Solana blockchain to shed some light on the latter. We will also try to give our view on the asset for the short/medium term.

Framework: Solana's GDP Explained in Depth

Solana GDP is calculated in a similar way to a country's GDP, consisting of two main components:

  • Protocol GDP: Composed of base, priority and voting fees, as well as native currency issuance as a reward for staking and Jito Tips, which are additional fees to prevent MEV (Maximum Extractable Value) attacks. This data is obtained through specific platforms such as Solana Explorer, which provides accurate and up-to-date details on each metric.

  • Application GDP: Includes revenue generated by decentralised applications in sectors such as DeFi, wallet-to-wallet payments and sector-specific applications. Tools such as Token Terminal and DeFiLlama provide detailed tracking, allowing you to quickly identify changes in usage and monetisation trends for specific applications.

This detailed breakdown enables an accurate and timely reading of the economic health of the network, facilitating informed investment decisions.

Once we are clear on the calculation methodology, we will begin to analyse the results by four-month period.

2024 Analysis: Correlation Between GDP Metrics and SOL Price Movements

A quarterly statistical analysis performed on data taken from Token Terminal shows a clear correlation (spearman correlation coefficient 0.76, statistically significant at p < 0.05) between increases in the GDP of the protocol and applications with respect to the subsequent SOL price appreciation. This is one of the main reasons for using this type of indicator.

The following image shows the results for the first four-month period of 2024, broken down between the GDP of the protocol and the GDP of applications. We can see that the predominant value is the issuance of native currency, which is normal in networks with the Proof of Stake concept. In the applications subgraph, the 'Wallet to Wallet' sector generates $40 million for the network.

In the second quarter data, we see a remarkable growth in the two GDP metrics. On the one hand, Protocol GDP increases to $2.59bn, mainly due to a clear increase in the amount of native currency blocked. On the Applications GDP side, we see a remarkable increase in the DeFi sector (Raydium, Meteora, Pumpfun.com) of more than 100%. It goes from $33 million to almost $78 million in a single quarter.

Let's look at the third quarter now. At the price level, the asset has been in a fairly wide range, but has ended up moving upwards by about 5%. On the GDP metrics, there is a very significant decrease in the total value of Solana issued, probably produced by a decrease in the amount locked in to secure the network. On the application side, we see clear declines in sectors such as DeFi and Wallet to Wallet.

Looking at the last quarter, we see very interesting facts. The GDP of the protocol increases from $1.11 bn to $2.38 bn with the doubling of all its main components. Jito Tips grew by 400% compared to the previous quarter. Fees double for base and voting fees. Priority fees also grew by 400%. In terms of protocol metrics, DeFi and Untagged Applications grew by almost 470%.

On a price level, the asset appreciates by 50% over the quarter, but peaks at 80% in just 3 months. This is for me a first indication that when transaction fees, Jito Tips and especially application transaction fees increase massively, the value of the asset appreciates accordingly by an increase in its transactional velocity. Value moves!

By closely monitoring these metrics, we can anticipate sharp market movements both upwards and downwards. If the data reflects these kinds of increases, it is because users are demanding more of the native asset, which is necessary to interact within the network, with any application that is based or runs on top of it. It could be seen as economies of scale when such increases in smart contract interactions are deployed on the network.

Current Outlook - What to expect in 2025?

We will now analyse the performance of the asset in terms of price and metrics for the year 2025. As mentioned at the beginning of this article, the price has fallen 55% from its all-time high. In terms of metrics, we see that all but the Solana issue value have fallen considerably. This leads us to a clear reflexive conclusion, when fees and application metrics increase very significantly, the issuance value of the coin is reduced because the different agents prefer to sell their assets or move them to other applications because the opportunity cost of leaving them blocked to validate the network is greater than the profitability they could achieve with their asset in another type of application. For me, a big component of price evolution (not counting exogenous variables such as Bitcoin, macroeconomics, or global economic cycles) is the formation of behavioural cycles. If the locked-in monetary value, reflected by the issuance of native currency, rises sharply, it means that hard times are coming for the asset. If, on the other hand, that value decreases (note that it will never decrease that much because the price itself influences that value) and application activity increases considerably, the asset will certainly appreciate.

Using historical trends and current GDP trajectories, we propose these scenarios for 2025:

  • Bullish Scenario: If DeFi revenues roughly double to $150 million in Q2 2025 and issuance remains stable, a realistic target price would be between $350-$400 per SOL.

  • Bearish Scenario: If application revenues stagnate or fall below $50 million quarterly, and issuance increases significantly, downward pressure towards $120-$150 levels could be expected.

Solana's price movements reflect real economic value, closely tied to network GDP metrics. Investors should use this framework to overcome speculative noise by strategically positioning themselves based on clear data-driven signals.

data.title: Tradeweb Government Bond Update - March 2025
data.description:
Global government debt markets experienced a sell-off in March, with yields increasing across the board. Those for Germany’s 10-year Bund saw the biggest move, rising by 34 basis points over the month to finish at 2.73%.

On March 4, the leader of the CDU/CSU Conservatives, Friedrich Merz, announced plans to loosen the country’s ‘debt brake’ to allow for higher defence spending. According to preliminary data from the Federal Statistical Office, the German inflation rate slowed to 2.2% in March, marking the lowest reading since November 2024.

On March 6, the European Central Bank cut its key rate by 25 basis points to 2.5%. Italy’s 10-year benchmark note yield increased by 33 basis points to 3.86% at month-end. The country’s inflation rate accelerated to 2% year-over-year in March, up from 1.6% the month prior. Meanwhile, the HCOB Italy Manufacturing PMI fell to 46.6 from 47.4 in February.

In neighbouring France, the yield on the 10-year government bond rose by 22 basis points to 3.45%, after reaching a three-year high of 3.6% on March 6. The annual inflation rate in the country remained unchanged at 0.8% in March, holding at its lowest level since February 2021.

Elsewhere in Europe, the UK 10-year Gilt yield climbed 22 basis points to close at 4.68%. The GfK Consumer Confidence rose slightly by one point to -19 in March, while the S&P Global UK Manufacturing PMI registered at 44.9, the lowest reading in 17 months.

“It was a busy month in the UK for fiscal policy and the publication of economic data. On March 26, Chancellor Rachel Reeves delivered the country’s Spring Statement, and the UK 10-year Gilt yield jumped by six basis points from 4.72% at 12:44pm to 4.78% at 12:54pm. On the same day, it was announced that the UK inflation rate fell more than expected to 2.8% in February, down from 3% the month prior. At the moment, 45 basis points worth of cuts by the Bank of England are being priced in over the next five meetings as implied by pricing on Tradeweb,” said Roderick Joniaux, Head of European Government & Supranational Products at Tradeweb.

Similar to its French equivalent, the Japanese 10-year government bond yield reached a three-year high of 1.59% on March 27, but ended the month at 1.49%, 12 basis points higher than February. On March 18, the Bank of Japan announced it would be holding its key interest rate at 0.5%. The annual inflation rate fell to 3.7% in February from a two-year high of 4% the month prior.

In Australia, the Westpac–Melbourne Institute Consumer Sentiment Index posted a 4% increase in March to 95.9 from 92.2 in February. The yield on the country’s 10-year benchmark note rose nine basis points to 4.36% at the end of March.

Its Canadian equivalent climbed seven basis points over the month to finish at 2.97%. The annual inflation rate in Canada jumped to 2.6% in February from 1.9% in January, which is the highest reading in eight months.

Finally, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield increased by just one basis point in March to 4.24%. On March 19, the Federal Open Market Committee voted to hold its interest rate in a range between 4.25%-4.5%, where it has been since December 2024.

The annual inflation rate in the U.S. eased to 2.8% in February from 3% in January, while the country added 151,000 jobs in February, up from a downwardly revised 125,000 in the prior month.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Market Reaction To Liberation Day - This Is Just The Beginning
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA and Francesco Pesole

Treasuries continue to view tariffs as a bigger immediate risk for activity

Donald Trump spoke of 10% tariff across the board before he was elected president. He’s kept true to that, imposing a blanket tariff. But he’s overlayed that with additional tariffs on a wide group of countries that the US views as already implicitly placing tariffs on US exports. See more detail here. Basically, the 10% tariff gets amplified to something closer to 20-25%, on average. To the extent that this places upward pressure on US domestic prices, there is clear negative tint for bonds. But the dominant reaction is one of apprehension on what this all means for activity, and hence, another push lower in market yields has been the main outcome.

At least for now we're still looking lower in yield. The only caveat is the floor we identify at around 3.5% for front-end rates (e.g. 1mth SOFR). That, plus a 50bp 10yr swap spread, pitches 4% as something of a floor for the 10yr Treasury yield. If Treasuries really want to rally, that "floor" needs to be lower. To get lower, we'd need to have a greater recession risk. That's the direction of travel, albeit the case for outright recession has not been fully made as of yet.

Risk-off has been a dominant play in recent weeks, and little there to change that

As a one-day trade, we've in part taken our cue from the risk asset space, and in particular equity markets. We don't love doing this, as we think the bond market is "smarter" and more long term in thinking. The impact reaction has been for lower equities, as priced from the futures. That's about the right reaction, but still remarkable as much of what was delivered was in a way anticipated. The picture being painted from a generic market sense is, in consequence, quite negative.

Anticipate further fallout over coming days. But distilling through the fog, it does feel that Treasury yields are in a mood to test lower still for a bit, trading off the growing recession risk being priced from a whole array of weak-leaning survey evidence, with tariffs acting as a tipping point. This needs to be disproved by events before Treasuries can start to latch on to the sticky inflation / elevated fiscal deficit narrative that sustains a bearish tint that can come back into play in due course.

Look for convergence of Treasuries to lower yielders, while higher yielders can ease lower

In terms of the international context, expect a period of reflection. To the extent that US yields fall in consequence, there is a downward effect on global rates, and a tightening versus lower yielders. Versus higher yielders, there is room being made for those rates to ease lower. No new news on Mexican tariffs, and Brazil was listed with a 10% tariff, which in net terms is a positive outcome for Latam.

For Asia, it a more troubling complex. China now averages at a 54% tariff, while Vietnam, Korea, India, Indonesia and Thailand are up for special extra tariffs mostly in the area of 25-45%. Then in Europe, a selection of countries are now on a 20% tariff, and we need to await retaliatory measures. Overall, there are negative kick-backs which likely mean a risk-off and lower yields combination dominating. See more detail on the tariff-impacted macro risks for Europe here.

Expect a degree of correlation in terms of performance ahead. So risk-off should be a common theme and should lower market rates. Europe is the most likely to buck this trend further forward as fiscal spending plans get fast-tracked in the wake of a Trump that's not for changing. This can push yields up in Europe. Similar for Japan in terms of yield. At the other exteme, the likes of Mexico and Brazil can see market rates being pulled lower by lower Treasury yields.

Asia EM FX the biggest loser

The currency market has faced major volatility around the US tariff announcement. The headlines suggesting the base rate for all countries was set at 10% sent the dollar tumbling, but country details unveiled shortly after triggered a broad rebound of the greenback. CAD and MXN have held up gains better than others thanks to USMCA exemption from reciprocal tariffs, while the yen remained the favourite hedge as risk sentiment soured.

The worst-hit region by this tariff announcement is undoubtedly Asian EM. Some Asian currencies are not trading at the time of writing, and we’ll need to wait a few hours before we can fully assess the fallout in the FX market. USD/CNH has strengthened following China's 34% tariff designation - one of the highest rates imposed - and yuan proxies AUD and NZD have shown some weakness, even if modest in magnitude. We anticipate broader Asian EM currencies will face downward pressure as trading resumes.

Tariffs open interesting relative value opportunities

Two critical aspects deserve attention from an FX perspective. First, this represents just the opening chapter of an evolving narrative. The US administration has signalled flexibility in tariff negotiations, and the full width of the currency fallout will primarily depend on individual countries' diplomatic and trade leverage, as well as on the US will to tolerate the negative implications and focus on collecting tariff revenue.

This leads to our second takeaway: the focus may well shift from broad dollar movements to relative value opportunities. It is abundantly clear by now that this was not the kind of event that would turn the tide for the dollar, and data can still prove to be the major driver of USD direction. But different countries’ abilities to negotiate away from tariffs can generate compelling relative value opportunities across G10 and EM FX.

We have pointed repeatedly at European currencies as vulnerable, and we still see downside risks as Trump has been vocal about growing hostility towards the EU, which may result in smaller trade negotiation room for the bloc. Yet, pre-tariff announcement euro strength emerged following reports of EU Commission plans for economic support measures. Thursday's anticipated details - potentially including progress on common debt instruments and reduced intra-EU trade barriers - warrant close attention before confirming our cautious EUR/USD outlook.

What about Trump’s weaker dollar hopes?

From an economic fundamental perspective, “liberation day” is a bullish dollar event. Indeed, the Treasury has indicated this is the peak for tariff threat and things can only improve from here via trade negotiations, but that does not explain by itself the dollar’s inability to rally. The real question is how long tariffs will stay in place, and the currency market retains a sanguine view on that. One way to interpret this optimistic stance is that inflationary pressures from higher import costs might accelerate trade dispute resolutions.

Looking ahead, Trump's well-documented preference for a weaker dollar adds another layer of complexity to future trade negotiations. We answered ten questions on how a Mar-A-Lago accord to devalue the dollar might look like in this note. We’ll see whether once the inflationary tariff shock has been absorbed Trump will turn the focus on weakening the dollar.

We still think the radical change in global trade relationships in the first few months of Trump’s presidency argues for a strengthening of the dollar across the board, but if US data keeps deteriorating and the US is forced to water down its tariff threat, the dollar would weaken without any artificial intervention. In this environment - and given the risks of more equity losses and lower long-end US yields - the yen may well emerge as the most attractive currency in G10 in the near term.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Archer-Daniels-Midland: Low Margins And Returns Make The Company A Hold
data.description:
Introduction

Archer-Daniels-Midland (NYSE:ADM) is a leader in the food industry with a rich history dating back to 1902. The company is part of the ABCDs, a group consisting of the four major agricultural commodity trading companies. ADM now has more than 327 plants and 520 crop procurement facilities worldwide. It processes cereal grains and oilseeds into a wide range of products, which makes it the "supermarket to the world".

ADM has become crucial in the global food supply chain, bridging the gap from farmers to markets worldwide. Outside of production, the company also owns ADM trucking and the American River Transportation company, which vertically integrates the company with easy logistics.

Yet, despite its important role in each of our meals, the company has lost over 23% over the past year due to various factors like the broad industry trend and commodity market pressure. That’s why I want to dive into the company to see if it’s worth it at these prices, or if the company has more room to fall.

Segment Overview

ADM splits its operation into three segments: AG Services and Oilseeds, Carbohydrate Solutions, and Nutrition.

Ag services and Oilseeds are the largest segment by revenue, with around $66.5B in sales in 2024. It is the bedrock of ADM’s operation, which is responsible for the sourcing, merchandising, transportation, and storage of agricultural raw materials as well as the processing of many oilseeds into oils and protein meals. It has assets such as a net of silos, ports and processing plants with a commodity trading and risk operation. This segment has faced some headwinds as of late due to low commodity prices and reduced export demand. This has slimmed already low margins as oversupply and weak global markets wreak havoc on the ABCDs. The segment is notoriously volatile due to crop prices and trade dynamics.

The carbohydrate solutions segment focuses on processing corn and wheat into various products such as starches, ethanol, and sweeteners. It’s also responsible for the infamous corn syrup, but also bioethanol, rendering the segment important for many clients such as the energy sector. The segment has also suffered from lower demand, yet it has benefitted from steady industrial demand and lower exposure to commodity swings.

Nutrition is the smallest segment, but it has become a growth driver for ADM as it produces specialty ingredients like the now popular plant-based proteins, probiotics, and other health-focused products. The segment targets alternative diets and pet food, which have the potential to provide higher margins than Ag services and Oilseeds. The segment was the one impacted by the accounting challenges in 2024, but despite that, it slightly grew from 2023. It has been hit by higher production costs and sparked some of the current investor skepticism.

Market Dynamics

The sector has been heavily influenced by commodity price swings, with prices remaining low for soybeans, wheat, and corn due to lower demand from key markets like China. For example, soybean crush profits declined nearly 50% YoY, which does not favor the company’s reliance on commodity trading, leaving limited pricing power.

The biofuel market has also faced some uncertainty, while it was touted as a growth driver for ADM. CI options are now becoming the norm, pressuring the soy business. That’s why the guidance is so low, due to weaker renewable diesel margins. Yet, sustainable aviation fuel might be the knight in the shining armor for the company for the segment, as demand might reach 10 MPTA by 2030.

The current worldwide trade dynamics are also impacting ADM, as recent tariffs plus the US-China tensions are touching all agricultural products and export volumes. However, the company has just signed an agreement with Mitsubishi Corp exploring agricultural collaboration that could boost ADM’s Asia presence.

Valuation

Currently, ADM has a nice valuation score according to Seeking Alpha with a B+. It currently has a P/E GAAP, 35.81% lower than the sector median (Non-GAAP is even lower at 10.17). EV/EBITDA is high at 11.09, in line with the industry. P/B is close to par at 1.04, significantly lower than the sector median of 2.59. The peers comparison could have been more relevant if Cargill and Louis Dreyfus were public, alas, we have to content ourselves with what we have.

*CTVA's P/E is 51.58; table was reduced for readability.

As mentioned earlier, most of the industry has been hit by headwinds; therefore, most have seen growth stalling or even depreciating. ADM's revenue has been growing at a slow 2.62% CAGR since 2015, but Net Income declined at a -0.3% CAGR rate in the same period.

In this case, Ingredion (INGR) stands out as it focuses on specialty products like starches, sweeteners, and plant-based ingredients that all cater to premium food that have higher margins and are less dependent on commodity prices. The company has a blend of operations comparable to ADM's growth drivers, showing us that high growth in the sector can be obtained.

Looking at profitability, ADM certainly does not have the most eye-watering margins due to the nature of the business. Margins have stayed relatively stable outside of the post-COVID commodity boom.

Returns metrics do not bode well either, as the commodity focus dilutes ROE and ROA as profits are spread across a huge network of assets. Current restructuring efforts, which included around 700 layoffs, incurred short-term costs, further eroding the bottom line.

All things considered, yes, ADM is cheap, but the weak margins and the low returns give us a reason to have doubts about investing in the company. The market is in a downturn, and no one knows if and when prices will turn back in favor of the ABCDs.

Conclusion

I would rate ADM as a hold in today's market, as there is too much unknown and volatility for the industry to spin out consistent net income. ADM's high dividend yield might entice investors to wait out the market downturn, but long-term, I don't see the company as a major compounder.

data.title: VUN:CA ETF: A True Total U.S. Equity ETF For Canadians
data.description:
What is VUN:CA?

The Vanguard U.S. Total Market Index ETF (TSX:VUN:CA) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) with a NAV of $11.5Bn CAD that invests in American listed stocks. While there are other total market index funds in Canada, I like VUN:CA and think it's the best representative of the total U.S. market. VUN:CA is very liquid and has a small expense ratio, making it an easy way to get U.S. equity exposure in Canada. This article seeks to compare this offering with some other larger American fund offerings, notably XUU:CA and other currency hedged total market ETFs like VUS:CA and XUH:CA.

VUN:CA's benchmark target is the CRSP US Total Market Index and after further review, I believe that VUN:CA is worth the investment. The fund has not materially deviated from its benchmark, while also presenting perfect representative tracking, with a Beta of 1 and an R-squared value of 1. While there are other similar alternatives, if investors want to get exposure to U.S. equities using Canadian dollars in a fund that truly represents the U.S. market, VUN:CA is a solid choice.

Fund Breakdown

VUN:CA was launched on August 2, 2013, by Vanguard with a plan to create a diversified, easily tradable fund that invested in U.S. companies of all sizes to track the market. The ETF has successfully tracked the total U.S. market for over 11 years now, and has been a clean way to get a mix of large, mid, and small-cap exposure. Granted, this index has trailed the large-cap S&P 500 over the past several years, but it's set out what it said it would do.

Additionally, the fund has lots of liquidity, with daily volumes averaging over 73,000 shares traded, which means ~$8MM CAD of volume per day. This is an important note, as many funds in Canada, given the lack of institutional and retail ownership, are thinly traded, leading to higher bid/ask spreads and mispricing the ETF units against the underlying holdings. Overall, the structure of the fund is solid and investors have been receptive to buying it to get U.S. large cap exposure.

Other types of competitors include currency hedged total market ETF's like Vanguard U.S. Total Market Index ETF (CAD-Hedged) (VUS:CA) and iShares Core S&P U.S. Total Market Index ETF (CAD-Hedged) (XUH:CA), but I prefer the unhedged versions. Currency hedging costs and performance drag can play a role in underperformance, as CAD-hedged ETFs use currency derivatives to eliminate USD/CAD exchange rate fluctuations. Over time, these hedging costs and inefficiencies can drag down returns. When the USD appreciates against CAD, unhedged ETFs benefit because their U.S. holdings become more valuable in Canadian dollars.

The flip side is that the opposite could occur when CAD strengthens, potentially allowing hedged ETFs to outperform - but it's not a clean switch as the funds rebalance daily, and not in real time. So their outperformance if CAD strengthens would occur but given the use of trading desk who need approvals from the risk team at large institutions, some drag can happen. The Canadian dollar has fallen against the USD over the last few years due to factors like interest rate differentials, economic growth, and global risk sentiment. Since XUU:CA and VUN:CA hold U.S. stocks in USD, their value in CAD increased when the USD strengthened, boosting returns. Meanwhile, hedged ETFs don't receive this benefit since they neutralize currency movement, missing out on gains when CAD depreciates. If investors think that the CAD will roar back, then by all means invest in XUH:CA or VUS:CA, but I prefer the unhedged option - and the returns difference has been notable.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing VUN:CA's portfolio, there are some important differences in the portfolio breakdown to SPY. As of Feb. 28, 2025, the portfolio somewhat resembles XUU:CA, with top holdings like AAPL, MSFT and NVDA and sector diversification similar. But I think the fund's expense ratio of 0.15%, which is actually a little bit higher than XUU:CA, is worth paying for given the differentiation and true meaning of the total U.S. stock market. The fund facts below note that the fund tracks all major sectors and portfolio weights closely to the benchmark - about 68% large-cap, 20% medium-cap, and 12% small-cap. I appreciate this, as it is a fair representation of the market in the U.S.

Meanwhile, XUU:CA, while a bit cheaper from an expense ratio perspective and boasting similar returns (actually up ~1% from VUN:CA over a 5yr period), is not a true total market index fund. Here's the fund facts breakdown of what it invests in.

And when looking at the first two ETFs, which make up over 93% of the weight, the market-cap breakdowns show 90%+ large-cap exposure. I don't care if it has slightly outperformed VUN:CA over the 5-year timeframe. I simply don't believe that the U.S. market is ~88% large-cap companies, and it's a fundamental reason why I own VUN:CA in Canada - it simply tracks the U.S. market in a more appropriate way. I recognize that public companies are biased to be larger than your mom & pop shops in the Midwest, but by not even trying to weight smaller companies accordingly is why I don't buy XUU:CA even with its strong performance... I can buy a separate large-cap ETF to track S&P 500.

VUN:CA has a Sharpe ratio of 0.63, which measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking, and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better, though around 1 is fine as an investment.

Risks & Bottom Line

The key risk in investing in this fund is that the American economy slides, which would make the investment depreciate and effect both large and smaller companies. Recently, the market has seen shaky returns, and I can't predict what will happen next. But the bottom line is that this fund is an easy way to get U.S. equity exposure in Canada. The fund has a low expense ratio that is comparable to peers, the fund is very liquid to the tune of $8MM CAD traded per day, and it actually weights in smaller companies. While XUU:CA is another unhedged option, it is almost a fully large cap fund, making it useless - you can simply buy ZSP:CA or any other S&P 500 tracking Canadian ETF's.

data.title: ASML On The Verge Of Losing EUV Monopoly
data.description:
Investment thesis: Given the significant recent tech sector selloff, I have been looking at potential bargains, some of which I identified and already acted on. Despite a significant decline of roughly 40% from all-time highs, ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) is a tech company that does not look as attractive as one might assume, given recent news this month, such as that of Chinese breakthroughs on building their own domestic high-performance lithography machines. With Huawei testing its patented EUV machine this year and planning on mass production by next year, assuming all will go well, it seems that ASML's global monopoly on high-end chip-producing machines may be coming to an end. Given China's ability to mass-produce and do it cheaper, the market could potentially be flooded with Huawei's EUV lithography machines, as well as other competing products, faster than most might expect. There are also rising trade barriers that pose a risk to ASML's future sales prospects. Given its current valuation, the growing risks to ASML's prospects are far from accounted for by the market, which is why I currently have it as a hold, looking for a better initial entry point.

ASML is still priced for healthy profit growth, even though financial results suggest slowing revenue growth and lower profit margins.

ASML had strong revenue growth at the start of the decade, however, last year's results showed a dramatic decline in its growth trajectory.

For this year, its revenue guidance is between 32-35 billion euros, which suggests a re-acceleration even if it comes in on the low-end, unless it misses the mark.

For the last quarter of 2024, ASML's net income was about 31% higher than the same quarter from a year earlier. A net income of 2.7 billion euros on revenues of 9.3 billion euros amounts to a healthy profit margin of 29%. It is perhaps one of the best indicators of the benefit that ASML derives from its high-end lithography monopoly on the global stage. It should be noted that net income for the whole of 2024 was about 3.5% lower compared with the prior year.

Current liabilities increased by about 25% in 2024, compared with the prior year to over 20 billion euros. Its net interest expenses declined by 20 million euros year over year to just 20 million euros, which is an insignificant factor in its overall financial results, given its total revenues for the year of over 28 billion euros. ASML's low debt servicing costs stand out as perhaps the most attractive aspect of the company's financial results within the context of what might be coming in the second half of the decade. It is one of the factors that can help sustain ASML's share buyback program, which can offer share price support for investors, even as growth prospects may dwindle. It is also a solid financial foundation from which it may be considered to be in a good position to compete with an emerging Chinese tech ecosystem that does not include ASML, and it is supposedly developing similar capabilities, catching up to ASML's technological advantage in the field.

The technological containment strategy against China is a failure, and it may have made things worse for ASML.

When China released in "Made in China 2025" plan a decade ago, the realization set in that China would not be content with simply serving as the world's factory. It wants to have its place in the global tech ecosystem. The containment policy started soon after, starting with the takedown of Huawei, which the first Trump administration felt it would have a significant impact, which it did in the short term.

Huawei retreated for a while, and it seemed that it would just fade away, but with help from the Chinese government, it became an important platform for China's overall strategy of becoming either an important participant in the global tech ecosystem or a competitor to the current Western-dominated one. Last year, it returned to China's domestic smartphone market, clawing back its market share from the likes of Apple (AAPL) and others. It seems that by this time next year, it is planning to offer a viable alternative to ASML's EUV machines if all goes according to plan.

It is important to remember that resolving the high-end chip production capacity issue is just a small part of China's larger plan to build a global challenger to the current technological ecosystem. Parallel with these efforts, it came up with what seems to be an overall better AI tool in terms of costs & efficiency, in the form of Deep Seek and other domestic advances in the broader IT field. These advancements are designed to be integrated and packaged into end products for consumers. In other words, Huawei hardware may come with DeepSeek embedded in the product, as it will probably be the case with most domestic Chinese communication devices. What this may all mean for ASML is that consumers around the World who might want to use AI services, such as DeepSeek, will probably opt for Chinese-made devices that will be compatible with such programs. Those Chinese devices will increasingly not use chips manufactured with ASML machines, but rather Chinese ones.

The integrated global market scenario.

The process of technological decoupling has been a concept explored for some years now. It generally ends with the same conclusion each time, namely that it will have a net negative impact on the global economy. As I pointed out already, it is also a net negative for tech companies like ASML, since it hypothetically restricts future access and opportunities to the Chinese and other markets. It is questionable whether ASML and other Western-based tech companies would benefit if the complete splitting of the world into two separate tech ecosystems is averted.

Much still depends on how far along the likes of Huawei and others are in terms of challenging ASML's near monopoly in the high-end segment of chipmaking equipment. If reports of Huawei being on the verge of potentially mass-producing its own EUV machines as early as next year will prove to be true, this might be the last year that ASML will hold a monopoly on advanced EUV technology. Next year, its sales can indirectly be impacted by the presence of Huawei's machines, as companies that currently buy those EUV machines will brace for lower global sales. Thus they will scale back investments. Further down the line, Huawei & ASML machine sales may come into competition with each other in existing as well as proliferating markets, such as India. Profit margins, which are healthy for ASML at the moment, are likely to diminish as it will attempt to support sales and maintain global market share by lowering prices.

Investment implications:

  • Not all of ASML's sales come from EUV machines; older tech sales are holding up alright, despite growing competition worldwide.

As the data points above show, net sales are still dependent on China to a great extent, and less than half of its sales are EUV machines. Contrary to commonly held notions, most of the world's chip demand is not for the high-end top-of-the-line chips, but rather for chips produced with older capacities that end up in cars, airplanes, missiles, and other bulkier products. ASML is still competitive in the lower-end DUV segment, but there is already much competition around the world that offers alternatives, therefore, its growth prospects in that segment are increasingly limited.

Looking at ASML's sales by market, China is an important customer, despite current sales restrictions in place for the high-end EUV machines. Besides Huawei's EUV breakthrough, there is also a company linked to Huawei, called SiCarrier that is rising as a potentially dominant company in China's DUV market. It seems that next year will be a crucial one for ASML, because Chinese competitors in China, its largest market by far in 2024, are looking to produce a strong challenge to its market share. Beyond next year, things can potentially become a lot worse.

  • Risks to my thesis.

If most of the headline-making advances that are being trumpeted out of China will prove to be exaggerations, we are likely to find out in the next year or two at the most. In that case, my strategy to wait out a further decline in ASML's stock price might prove to be an opportunity missed. At the same time, an investment timed to coincide with a lower P/E ratio, which is what I am aiming for, may prove to be risky. In a worst-case scenario, Chinese competitors will exceed all expectations in all ways, and as a result, they might flood the Chinese as well as the global markets with their equipment, sooner and faster than one might expect.

  • Realistic expectations may produce a potentially lucrative investment opportunity if well-timed.

Because of the negative risks associated with growing Chinese competition, as well as perhaps a less robust global economy for the foreseeable future, ASML's current valuation, with a P/E of around 26, seems out of place with logical risk/reward considerations, despite the roughly 40% decline in its share price.

Between the rising domestic capabilities of Chinese companies to match ASML technologically, and the deteriorating global trade environment, ASML could potentially lose the Chinese market, which is currently its most important market by far. This is the risk that needs to be priced into its valuation and share price, which is yet to be fully priced in.

I am looking for a buying opportunity if ASML's P/E ratio drops below 20. It would be a good initial entry point, with an intent to add more if it drops further toward 15. At the moment, with a steep drop in its share price already on the books, instinct would dictate that a bet on an upswing may be in the cards. It may be the case in the shorter term, but as the trade issues and the China tech advancement issues get further crystallized, and with it the implications for ASML, there is a good chance that we will see further significant downside for its share price. Once these risks are priced in, ASML can become a buy, on its sophisticated technology that as of right now is still ahead of all competition, a solid market in the shape of the US-dominated alliance of Western and other mostly developed nations, where Chinese technologies are likely to be mostly kept out of the market, as well as a solid financial base. For now, I intend to keep watch, looking for the opportunity to buy in at a lower valuation level to arise. If it does not, I am willing to live with not having invested. ASML is a solid company, but the growing risks cannot be ignored.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PPLT: Gold Has Been On Fire This Year, Platinum Might Be Next
data.description:
Thesis

Platinum is a precious metal alongside gold and silver, and is commonly known for its high durability, resistance to corrosion, and use in jewelry and electronics. Precious metals have been on a tear in the past year, with gold and silver posting eye-watering returns:

The SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) is up 38% in the past year, while the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) is up 33.9%. These are astounding figures, especially when considering the backdrop of high rates. Since metals do not produce any sort of yields, high interest rate environments are usually correlated with downside pressure on precious metals.

In today's article we are going to explore the abrdn Physical Platinum Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:PPLT), go through its structure and composition, and articulate why platinum might follow the same path as gold as silver.

Fund composition

Unlike other commodity ETFs which employ futures, PPLT is backed by actual physical platinum held by a custodian, in this case ICBC Standard. The physical vaults are located in London, and the fund actually has a list with the platinum bars they hold, including serial numbers and origin.

The issue with many commodities is that they cannot be physically held due to various constraints. Some are perishable (think agricultural commodities), while others have significant physical constraints (think oil and natural gas). Precious metals, however, do not exhibit this issue, thus physically backed ETFs are possible. The utilization of a physically held asset eliminates any futures linked issues around rolling contracts or market dynamics (contango versus backwardation, etc.).

The physical aspect of the holdings might also be appealing to investors who distrust the financial system, loathing investment banks and the counterparty risk inherent in many derivative transactions.

What is platinum actually used for?

Although it is categorized as a precious metal, platinum has real life utilizations:

Most of the utilization for platinum is in the auto industry and the jewelry sector. Platinum is a known component of combustion engine catalytic converters, while it also serves many functions in jewelry composition. Kindly note that while historically, it was also held for investment, that figure decreased in 2022 as interest rates rose.

The move toward electric vehicles is well documented, but the ultimate death of the conventional car is very far away. In fact, we are witnessing a transition to hybrid vehicles as we speak, rather than pure EVs. From this lens, the death of the demand from the auto sector is a decade away, if not more as far as platinum is concerned.

In the near term future, however, we are of the opinion that the investment category is going to perk back up, consistent with its historic pattern prior to rates moving higher in 2022/2023.

Correlations

While undoubtedly gold is the preferred store of value for central banks, silver has seen a substantial bid as well on the back of said gold flows. In this section, we are going to explore platinum's historic correlations to gold and silver, and try to imply if PPLT can follow the same parabolic move as GLD.

Looking back from 2010 onwards, we get the following correlations:

PPLT has a similar correlation to GLD and SLV, lingering around the mid 60% figures. From an annual standpoint, we can see a clear pattern:

The metal maintained its high mid-60s correlation for long periods of time, up until the Covid crisis. It then reverted to its historic levels, only to lag significantly the recent gold and silver rally.

Let us look at returns by year to get a sense of what happened during Covid and then more recently:

All metals were up during Covid, but PPLT lagged the monster move in gold and silver. The lower correlation therefore was due to a less positive move rather than anything else. Kindly keep in mind that while platinum is a store of value, during Covid it was affected by the significant decrease in auto demand, an aspect which can be seen from the demand table above as well.

Historically, the three metals are well correlated, with negative total returns during the same years, outside of 2024, when platinum was the only one lagging. If history is any guide, platinum is set to catch up on the monster move in the other metals from 2023 to 2025:

  • GLD total return: 58%
  • SLV total return: 36%
  • PPLT total return: -8.6%

If we use the same correlation factor as the historic one (i.e., roughly 60%), we should get an implied move of +34% using gold, and +21% using silver. Platinum therefore has a lot of 'catching up' to do.

Geopolitics and precious metals

One thing an investor has to be very cognizant of is geopolitics. Gold as a classic store of value is a trade which has been sparked by geopolitics. The Russian invasion of Ukraine and continued China - U.S. tensions play a very large role here. After Russian's actions, its central bank reserves held in dollars and euros have been frozen. While so far, only the interest due on those reserves has been taken and given to Ukraine, the entire reserves might be used to rebuild Ukraine post-conflict.

This state of affairs has made one aspect crystal clear for central banks - dollar and euro denominated assets are not necessarily a 'safe' store of value. Since said obligations can only clear through local clearing houses, they are subject to seizures under U.S. and European law. The only other clear-cut store of value is represented by precious metals, which can be physically held in Russia or China or any other country and serve as collateral for borrowings or be sold. Physical precious metals cannot be seized in the same fashion as other financial assets. While platinum is not as wide spread in the central bank community, it nonetheless possesses the same store of value qualities as its brethren gold and silver.

I am not a metals investor. Should I still consider PPLT?

The name of the game is to make money. Macro allocators are very good at identifying trends and moving to those pockets that generate gains during specific years. In equities land, there are momentum ETFs that do just that - they identify single name equities with the highest momentum, and add them to their portfolios.

When gold and silver have such significant positive momentum and platinum has a high correlation to those metals but has been lagging, the normal question to ask is whether platinum will follow. It is hard to time markets, thus calling for a top in gold or silver is very difficult to do. What is not difficult is identifying that platinum is historically cheap versus these two, and if history is any guide, it might need to do some 'catching-up'. In the same train of thought, even central banks might consider gold and silver expensive and might want to look at loading up on other physical precious metals, thus adding a bid for platinum.

Conclusion

PPLT is an exchange-traded fund that holds physical platinum. The metal is held in London vaults by a custodian, and thus the ETF is backed by a physical asset rather than futures. Platinum has had a strong historic correlation to gold and silver, but has lagged since 2023. A reversion to its historic correlation would imply a 20% plus up-move from here, given the gigantic rally in gold and silver in the past year. We are buyers of PPLT at the current price levels.

data.title: General Dynamics: Slow Growth With A Potential Market Change
data.description:
General Dynamics (NYSE:GD) is a company I have had on my radar for a long time, especially after their stock price has fallen by 10.22% through the past 6 months. In my eyes, this company is a recession-proof one, providing decent and steady growth, especially thanks to their defense revenue. But what if I were to believe that in the future, there will be much less conflict than there is now? Today, I have to rate the company as a HOLD, as I believe that in the far future, there will be much less conflict than there is now, as humanity matures.

Now to fully understand why my rating is as such, we will first make a market overview and check out the company's most recent Q4'24 highlights, to then analyze its fundamentals through our 9 Pillar Analysis, and finally calculate the company's intrinsic value through 4 of our intrinsic valuation models.

Introduction

First of all, I want to introduce what GD actually does. 53.00% of the company's estimated revenue comes from sales in defense, with the remaining 47.00% coming from Intelligence, state, and federal agencies, and commercial, with a 20.00%, 23.00% and 4.00% revenue share, respectively.

This is something I very much like about the company, as it is not purely dependent on a single sector. Furthermore, GD also operates in quite a few segments, with very evenly spread out revenue-generating abilities between them. Around 21.30% of the company's revenue comes from aerospace, both defense and civilian; 30.80% comes from marine systems, 19.00% comes from combat systems, and 28.90% comes from technologies.

One thing that can be taken both positively and negatively though is the geographical revenue split. In 2024, 83.80% of the company's revenue came from the USA, which on one hand is great, as it means that with the current Trump administration, it wouldn't be subject to tariffs. On the other hand, it means that most of the company's revenue-generating abilities aren't expanding past the US, and so if the US were to cut on military spending, which Trump has already proposed, and some cuts were already carried out, GD might experience a fall in its revenue, especially as more than 50.00% of its revenue comes from defense sales in the US.

Analysis

Now, as I am a long-term value investor, I like to look at things from an investing perspective of at least 30 years. Of course, in my eyes there will always be a need for defense, but in the long term, there might be less need than you think. Below you can see a graph depicting the battle death rates throughout the world declining quite drastically through the past 50+ years, even though since 1950, the world population has more than tripled.

Think about it: as less developed countries, such as some in Africa or some in the Middle East, which are now the main bearers of conflict, start becoming more developed, at some point reaching strong democracy like we have in Europe and the US, there will be much less demand and need for weaponry.

If you think about the two great wars we have had in the past 110 years, they were both between now democratic states, which we now wouldn't ever imagine fighting between each other ever again.

If my thesis were to be correct, in the next 50 years or even sooner, throughout the whole world, there would be almost no conflicts, erasing the need for most weaponry such as jets, battleships, submarines, etc. But again, that is just my speculation and my perspective. As already stated, defense makes up about half of the company's total revenue, meaning that if there was a decrease in the demand for weaponry, they would still have half of their revenue-generating assets. Furthermore, this huge change would be very gradual, taking decades, and so I believe that the company would take the correct steps towards shifting their focus to more civil machinery.

Q4'25 Earnings

But let's now throw my speculations behind us and take a look at the company's latest advances.

They reported their EPS as a beat by $0.12. It was expected at $4.03 and came in at $4.15. Regarding the revenue, that was as well a beat by $0.52B, reported at $13.34B while the expected revenue was $12.82B.

Now regarding the change in specific segments of the company, the Aerospace revenue grew by 30.00% upon the launch of the G700, with the aircraft sales up by 36.80% and services up by 17.40%.

The revenue from combat systems also grew YOY by 9.00%, with the Marine systems growing 15.00% and the Technology segment growing by 1.58%. So it is quite obvious that the company is prospering, with most of their segments growing at high single, to low double digits YOY.

9 Pillar Analysis

But as I have already mentioned, I am a long-term investor, so to really understand how well the company has been doing, and how well they are set to do in the future, let's jump to the 9 Pillar Analysis and check out their 5Y Fundamentals.

First things first, if you aren't familiar with the 9 pillars, let me quickly summarize this simple analysis model. Pillars 1 and 2 represent the 5-year Price-to-Earnings (PE) and Price-to-Free Cash Flow (P/FCF) ratios, respectively. Pillars 3, 4, and 5 illustrate the growth in revenue, net income, and free cash flow over the past 5 years. Pillar 6 focuses on the 5-year trailing profitability, while Pillar 7 highlights the change in shares outstanding. Pillar 8 tracks dividend growth, and Pillar 9 calculates the ratio of long-term liabilities to 5-year free cash flow minus dividends, which assesses a company's capacity to pay down debt within a reasonable period while still paying dividends.

Above, you can see that GD gets a decent rating of 8/9, with almost all the pillars sitting in the green.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

First, let's check out the three most important metrics, which are, in my eyes, revenue, net income and FCF growth. Over the past 5Y, the revenue has grown from $37.93B in 2020 to $47.72B in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of 4.80%.

Regarding the net income, that has been growing at a 5Y CAGR of 3.76%, increasing from $3.17B in 2020, to $3.78B in 2024. The EPS has also increased from $11.00 to $13.63 in the same time frame.

Finally, regarding the FCF, that has increased by 12.64% in the past 5 years, growing at a CAGR of 3.85% from $2.83B to $3.19B. So overall, you can see that GD is a slow grower, with their average financial CAGR sitting at 4.13% over the past 5Y. This gives us a general idea about how much the share price based on value should be growing on a yearly basis. But to understand better how the company is valued, let's take a look at pillars 1 and 2.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Regarding the 5Y PE ratio, it is sitting quite low at only 17.04 but has been growing at a CAGR of 4.80%, which means that investors have been paying more and more for the company's earnings through the past 5 years, with the PE currently sitting at 20.11, the highest it has sat at through the previously mentioned time period.

Regarding the P/FCF, it has been moving very similarly to the PE, with the 5Y average sitting at 15.85, growing at a CAGR of 4.78%, to now sit at 19.88.

So overall, I wouldn't say that GD is overvalued compared to their financials, as their PE and P/FCF are both sitting in decent ranges, but I do believe that they are gaining more and more premium from investors, which, when kept increasing at its historical rate, might make the company overvalued in the future and possibly lead to its stock price decreasing.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Now, one pillar I find extremely important is Pillar 9, which allows us to see how much LTL a company has compared to the FCF they generate. GD's LTL/5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting at a decent size of 1.35, meaning that the company does generate enough FCF to cover all their LTL in a safe amount of time while paying out their dividends.

Something I also like is the fact that GD has been decreasing their LTL at a CAGR of 4.05%, allowing them to, in the future, possibly return more value to investors through dividends or share buybacks, as well as become more stable in case of possible financial downturns.

Overall, the 9 Pillars are looking quite well. GD does seem to be keeping their fundamentals healthy by decreasing their debt, while keeping their profit margin quite stable at around 8.00% over the past 5 years

Furthermore, their 5Y dividend CAGR is sitting nice and high at 6.60%, and the company has a 5Y FCF payout ratio of only 41.27%, which means that while returning more and more value to its investors, they also have safe dividends with excess FCF to push into innovation, raising their dividends further, paying down debt, etc...

The only thing that bothers me slightly is the company's slow-growing financials, compared to the fast-growing price per net income and FCF.

Intrinsic Value

So now, let's figure out with certainty if GD is over or undervalued through our intrinsic value models, starting with the Graham's Formula.

Here, I will plug in the 5Y EPS sitting at $12.85 and project a YOY EPS growth rate into the next few years at 6.33%. This will give me an intrinsic value of $197.71, 27.88% lower than the current price of $274.14. But we still have three more models to go through, so let's jump to the Multiples Valuation.

In this valuation, I will compare GD to two of its competitors: Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman. I will take these peers' PE ratios and average them out to 19.11, lower than the PE of GD. Therefore, when we multiply this average by the current EPS of GD, sitting at $13.63, we will get an intrinsic value of $260.43.

Next, I want to look at the DCF model, where I will project a 10Y + FCF CAGR of 5.70%, and combining that with a discount rate of 8.00%, get an intrinsic value of $229.42.

Finally, for the DDM, I will project a YOY dividend growth rate of 6.00% into the future and get a value of $318.00, higher than the current price.

To get the ultimate intrinsic value, we will average out the 4 values we just calculated and get $251.39, which is 8.30% lower than the current price of $274.14. If I were to also add a safety margin of 10.00%, our acceptable buy price would sit at $226.25, which supports my HOLD rating.

Conclusion

In conclusion, based on General Dynamics' slower growth and slight overvaluation, I have to rate them as a HOLD. This rating is further supported by my long-term thesis that in the future there will be less need for defense machinery, which might lead to the company struggling slightly. I believe that if GD was to drop further, reaching at least our intrinsic value, I might rate them differently, but for now, my rating is as stated above.

data.title: A New Chapter For United States Steel: Growth, Risks And Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

The United States Steel Corporation (NYSE:X) is a true legend of the American industry with a long history that began in 1901. Today, U.S. Steel produces almost the entire range of steel products: hot-rolled and cold-rolled products, galvanized, sheets, pipes. Recent years have been a roller coaster ride for U.S. Steel, to put it mildly. 2020 turned out to be an expectedly unprofitable year for the company, and it is clear why there is a pandemic. But already in 2021, the company made a spectacular return: $1.74 billion in net profit, and revenue jumped to $20.28 billion. The year 2022 continued its positive trend, but since the end of that year, everything has started to change again: the market has cooled, prices have gone down. In 2023, the profit was already $895 million, and in 2024 it fell to $384 million. The reason is another round of decline in demand and prices for steel, revenue fell to $ 15.64 billion. The pipe industry was particularly hard hit: the average price of products collapsed by almost 40%, and this hurt by the end of the year. In December 2023, the company decided to buy Japanese Nippon Steel for $14.9 billion, at $55 per share. The deal is still in the process of being negotiated, but the fact itself speaks volumes — even in the downturn, this company remains strategically valuable for the global steel market.

Business model

By the end of 2024, the company's business is structured in four areas: these are Flat-Rolled — classic blast furnace plants in the USA, Mini Mill — more technologically advanced and efficient electric steelmaking facilities (primarily Big River Steel), Tubular — the direction of pipes for oil and gas, and the European division of USSE — the plant in Kosice, Slovakia, which operates on the local EU market.

In terms of revenue, the Flat-Rolled segment is still the main engine, accounting for approximately 61% of all revenue. This is all the sheet steel that automakers, builders, manufacturers of machinery and more buy. The Mini Mill, which includes the flagship Big River Steel, is still the second largest (about 13% of revenue), but it is already clear that this area is becoming a key one. Especially after the launch of the new BR2 plant in 2024. The European division, the Slovakia plant (USSE), brings in about 19% more, while the Tubular segment covers the remaining 6% by supplying pipes for oil and gas.

The main feature of the U.S. Steel business model is rigid vertical integration. In other words, the company extracts the raw materials itself, processes them and produces the final product. In 2024, it produced more than 22 million tons of pellets and 4.5 million tons of coke. This strategy greatly reduces vulnerability to price spikes for ore, coal, and other external raw material chaos.

The year 2024 turned out to be difficult for the company — all segments went into negative revenue. Flat-Rolled declined by 11% due to falling shipments, Mini Mill sank by 7%, and Tubular lost as much as 37% due to falling pipe prices. The European segment sank by 15%. In 2025-2026, we are likely to see an increase in the share of Mini Mill in the business structure, and the entire model will become noticeably more balanced: from the extraction of raw materials to the production of high-tech steel for the needs of the automotive industry, energy and infrastructure.

Competitors

U. S. Steel operates in the steel industry, one of the most volatile and competitive industries in the world. Global production, according to the World Steel Association, reached 1.88 billion tons in 2024, demand increased slightly by 1.7%, but the overall market mood remains subdued: the economy in developed countries is stalling, and China — which used to pull the market up — is now stagnating. The forecast for 2025 is an increase of about 1.2% (to 1.815 billion tons). In other words, the industry expects low growth in the near future (a global CAGR of 1-2%). Faster rates are predicted in some emerging markets: for example, outside of China, cumulative demand growth is estimated at 3.5% in 2024-25, with India leading the way (+8% in these years). But in developed countries, demand is increasing modestly (North America and the EU – about 1-3% per year), while in China it is stagnating and has probably reached a peak (zero growth is expected in 2024 and a decrease after).

Within the United States, where U.S. Steel has its main business, volumes account for only 4-5% of global output, but thanks to protective measures such as duties and quotas, the company retains a strong position — it is one of the three leaders in terms of volume, occupying 13-15% of the domestic market. On the global stage, of course, the scale of business is more modest — less than 1%, and here U.S. Steel competes with giants like China Baowu or Tata Steel (OTC:TATLY). Internally, the main opponents are Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) and Nucor (NUE). The former, like U.S. Steel, operate on an integrated model and are strong in the automotive industry. But Nucor and Steel Dynamics (STLD) are already a new school: they make steel in electric furnaces, move quickly, flexibly, and can maintain margins even in difficult periods because they have lower costs.

U.S. Steel has some advantages over its competitors in the form of its mines, vertical business integration and a wide product range, but the high share of fixed costs, union pressure and the need to quickly catch up with EAF competitors create significant pressure on the business, especially against the background of investors' expectations to see a quick return on recent investments. To stay in the game, the company needs to move forward without stopping, because the market is overheated, competition is fierce, and only those who know how to adapt faster than others survive here.

Financials

In 2024, U.S. Steel's revenue decreased by 13% to $15.64 billion, reflecting weak pricing conditions and declining shipments across the board. Flat-Rolled sank by $1.14 billion amid falling demand from the automotive industry and a decrease in volumes of almost 900 thousand tons. In Europe, through the USSE, revenue decreased by $542 million due to the recession and cheaper products. The pipe business became the most problematic: Tubular lost $581 million, almost 40% of last year's level, as the sharp pullback in prices after overheating in 2022 hit this segment hard.

At the same time, over the past five years, revenue has ranged from $9.7 billion in 2020 to a peak of $20.3 billion in 2021, which gives a CAGR of about 4% per year. The company has not published a clear guidance for 2025, but management says conditions are improving, especially in the second half of the year, based on the introduction of BR2 and restoration in pipes and Europe. I am putting in my forecast revenue of $16.38 billion (+4.7% YoY), and on the horizon until 2030 — growth to $18.57 billion, that is, a CAGR of about 3%. This pace looks realistic, especially if the new facilities are fully operational and the market does not fall into recession.

In 2024, business margins came under pressure — EBITDA was about $1.2 billion with a margin of 7.7%, which is almost three times lower than in 2022. The reasons are clear: steel prices have fallen, and costs — especially in the Flat-Rolled segment — have remained high due to underutilization of capacity and inflation. Operating profit was almost zero ($287 million), and net profit was $384 million, which corresponds to a net margin of only 2.46%. Nevertheless, the company remained in the black — unlike in past recessions when it easily went into losses.

As for 2025, the company planned to end the first quarter in the red (adjusted loss of about $115 million), but recovery is expected from the second half of the year. I expect EBITDA of $2.1 billion (margin ~12.8%) and net profit of ~$918 million, which is more than double the 2024 figures. The main factors are an increase in BR2 loading, stabilization of steel prices and economies of scale. By 2026, the consensus is that margins could reach an even higher level, especially if the U.S. Steel will achieve the stated savings of ~$150 per ton at the new facilities, which will give it a chance to maintain 8-10% of net margin even without a market boom.

In 2023-2024, the company invested in new facilities and continued to return capital to shareholders, which led to an increase in net debt to $2.89 billion. At the same time, the total debt remained stable at about $4.25 billion, and the increase in the debt burden was mainly due to a decrease in the cache to $1.37 billion. It is important that the debt structure remains healthy: almost all of the debt is long—term, the short—term portion is only $95 million, and interest payments are well covered by operating profit. Debt load ratios have increased, but are within acceptable limits — Net debt/EBITDA is about 2.4x at the end of 2024. The liquidity is high: in addition to the cache, there is access to more than $2.2 billion of credit lines.

The year 2024 was the peak year for U.S. Steel in terms of capital expenditures — the company invested $2.287 billion, directing most of these funds to the construction of BR2, the development of the Mini Mill segment and the modernization of the raw material base. More than 70% of all investments went to new mini-plants, which confirms the strategic focus on transformation. The CapEx ratio to revenue was about 14.6%, which is significantly higher than the industry norm and reflects the phase of active growth. However, a new chapter begins in 2025: management plans to drastically reduce investments to $1 billion, which is more than half the level of 2024. This will bring CapEx/Revenue back to the 6-7% range and free up cash flow, allowing the company to start reaping the benefits of previously completed projects.

U.S. Steel's free cash flow has been very volatile in recent years. With the growth of the investment program in 2023 and especially in 2024, the situation turned around: profits sank, CapEx remained high, and FCF became negative — minus $1.37 billion in 2024. This deficit was covered by the accumulated cache. In 2025, CapEx is expected to plummet to $1 billion, while operating flow will begin to recover. I expect an FCF of about $950 million in 2025. Judging by the comments of the management, the company is ready to switch from the investment mode to the return on capital phase. If the market does not fail, free flow will once again become a stable source of growth and an opportunity to reduce debt or pay shareholders.

In 2024, the dividend per share was $0.20 with a yield of about 0.47%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $45 million dollars for dividends, or $0.20 per share with a yield of 0.47%.

Valuation

The company's shares have been noticeably lagging behind market growth over the past year and have been in a narrow price range of about $30-40. I believe that stocks as a whole are close to their fair values and do not see any potential for their growth in the coming year.

Firstly, the company trades at a premium relative to its historical averages in terms of multipliers: the premium for P/E is 105%, and for EV/EBITDA it is 84.3%.

At the same time, the company trades near average values with peers in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S, EV/EBITDA multipliers, as can be seen in the table below. HPE looks undervalued relative to the average values among competitors: the company's P/E is 27.04x, with an industry average of 26.14x, and EV/EBITDA is 10.45x with an average of 12.54x.
